nativeRange.habitat: Warm, shallow, densely vegetated lowland waters: slow-moving rivers, backwaters, lagoons, ditches, and coastal marshes with abundant aquatic vegetation; tolerates slightly brackish conditions

decorPreferences: dense surface vegetation and floating plants (water lettuce, Amazon frogbit) for cover
emergent plants and tall background vegetation
driftwood and branches near the surface
minimal open water — heavily planted periphery with open central swimming lane

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in practice; wild diet consists almost entirely of live fish and occasionally large invertebrates. In captivity, requires a steady supply of live feeder fish (guppies, mollies, platies, small goldfish). Some individuals can be conditioned over time to accept large frozen/thawed whole fish (silversides, lance fish) and chunked raw fish flesh, but many stubbornly refuse anything that is not actively moving. Feeding dead or prepared foods is unreliable and specimens that refuse to convert may starve. Gut-loaded, nutritionally complete feeder fish should be used wherever possible to avoid nutritional deficiencies. Feeder fish from unknown sources carry disease risk; aquarist-bred feeders are strongly preferred.

goodWith: no tankmates recommended in a community setting
large robust livebearers of similar size in a species-only setup (with caution — cannibalism risk if size disparity exists)
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (virtually all common community fish)
guppies, mollies, platies, tetras, rasboras, danios, corydoras
juveniles with adults of the same species

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Belonesox belizanus. Standard FishBase species photo showing the characteristic elongated body and pike-like snout.

summary: The Pike Livebearer is the sole member of its genus and the only large obligate piscivore in the family Poeciliidae — a pike-like apex predator reaching 20 cm that gives birth to live young. Native to Central America; invasive in Florida. Specialist-only: requires live feeder fish and a species-only or single-specimen tank.
careNotes:
Belonesox belizanus is a uniquely specialized livebearer — the only member of genus Belonesox and the sole large obligate piscivore in the family Poeciliidae. Where all other poeciliids are micro-feeders or omnivores, the Pike Livebearer is a surface-ambush predator with a distinctly elongated pike-like body and large upturned mouth equipped with small but sharp teeth. Females are substantially larger than males, reaching up to 20 cm; males typically top out near 10–12 cm. Sexual dimorphism is pronounced even at a glance.

A tank of at least 200 L with a 120 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a single adult female; a breeding pair or small group requires 300 L or more. The species is a surface ambush predator that lurks motionless among dense vegetation before striking, so heavily planted tanks with floating cover are strongly recommended. Open water in the centre allows cruising; planted edges and surface cover provide naturalistic ambush zones and reduce stress.

Water conditions should be warm (24–30°C), moderately hard and alkaline (pH 7.0–8.5, gH 10–25). The species is native to coastal lowland habitats that can be slightly brackish, and tolerates a small salt addition (SG up to ~1.003) without issue — this can reduce disease pressure. Good filtration and regular water changes are essential, particularly given the high-protein live-fish diet that generates significant waste.

The primary challenge is diet: the Pike Livebearer is functionally a live-fish specialist. Aquarist-bred guppies, mollies, or platies make ideal feeder stock. Some individuals accept large frozen silversides or lance fish if slowly conditioned via hunger and movement simulation (e.g., dangling on a feeding stick), but conversion to dead foods is not guaranteed. Feeder fish sourced externally carry significant disease risk; establishing a dedicated feeder colony is the responsible approach.

Conspecific aggression and cannibalism are real concerns. Males are frequently harassed by females; a size-matched pair can be maintained for breeding, but size differences of any significance result in predation. Juveniles and adults must be separated immediately.

This species has established invasive populations in South Florida, introduced via aquarium releases into warm canals and ditches. Hobbyists must never release specimens into the wild.
breedingNotes: The Pike Livebearer is a livebearer (viviparous) and reproduces in the typical poeciliid fashion — females give birth to fully formed live fry. Gestation lasts approximately 4–6 weeks at optimal temperatures. Brood sizes are relatively small for a livebearer of this size, typically 20–100 fry depending on female size and age; larger, older females produce larger broods. Fry are born large by livebearer standards — often 1.5–2 cm — and are immediately capable predators, attempting to hunt from birth. Fry must be separated from parents immediately as both the female and male will consume them. A dedicated fry tank with small live food (newly hatched brine shrimp, small guppy fry) is required from day one. The male must typically be removed after mating to prevent female predation on him. Females can store sperm and produce multiple broods from a single mating. Selective breeding programs are rare given the specialist husbandry demands.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Belonesox belizanus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Atlantic slope lowlands from Mexico to Nicaragua), adult size (males to 10 cm, females to 20 cm), temperature range (26–30°C), pH (7.0–8.0), hardness tolerances, piscivorous diet details, breeding notes (gestation ~28 days, brood 20–100 fry), tank requirements, and conspecific aggression notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Belonesox belizanus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Poeciliidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (females to 20.5 cm TL, males smaller), native range (Mexico through Central America), invasive range (Florida, USA), diet (piscivore), and habitat (fresh and slightly brackish vegetated lowland waters). Confirmed monotypic genus Belonesox.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pike Killifish (Pike Livebearer)
additional.notes: Care guide confirming live-feeder dependency, specialist-only suitability, species-only tank requirement, size (females to 8 inches/20 cm), and temperature range (75–86°F / 24–30°C). Noted difficulty converting to frozen/prepared foods and invasive status in Florida.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Belonesox belizanus profile
additional.notes: Supplementary UK hobby source. Confirmed piscivore classification, livebearer biology, specialist care level, Central American origin, and the species\' distinction as the only large predatory poeciliid. Notes on feeding difficulty and aggression consistent with other sources.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Pike Livebearer species overview
additional.notes: Historical hobby source. Noted unique evolutionary position of Belonesox as the apex piscivore of the family Poeciliidae, live-fish feeding requirement, Florida invasive status, and breeding observations including large fry size at birth.

fish.breedingNotes: Livebearer; gestation ~4–6 weeks, 20–100 fry per brood depending on female size. Fry are born large (~1.5–2 cm) and immediately predatory. Immediate separation of fry and removal of male required to prevent cannibalism. Fry require live foods from birth.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Live-feeder-only piscivorous livebearer reaching 20cm. Will hunt and consume tankmates. Single-fish or species-only setup. Invasive in Florida via aquarium releases.
